full_response:
We treat the neighbourhood of the Schwarzschild horizon 

  r = r_s ≡ 2GM/c^2  

by the “flat–spacetime–near–an–acceleration–horizon’’ (Rindler) approximation.  Put  

  ρ ≡ 2√{r_s(r–r_s)},   a ≡ c^2/2r_s   (so a is the proper acceleration of a rocket that hovers just above the horizon).

In the Rindler chart (t,ρ) the metric is, to first order in ρ/r_s,  

  ds^2 ≃ –(aρ/c)^2 c^2dt^2 + dρ^2 + r_s^2 dΩ^2.

“People on the rocket’’ are at a fixed ρ = ρ_0 and are therefore uniformly accelerated with proper acceleration a_0 = c^2/ρ_0.  
“ You ’’ are released from rest with respect to the rocket, so after that you are in free fall; in the locally flat (T,X) Minkowski coordinates,

  T = ρ sinh(at/c), X = ρ cosh(at/c) ,

your world-line is simply a straight timelike line through the origin:

  X – X_1 = (T – T_1),                                         (1)

and you cross the Rindler horizon X = ±T (the true black-hole horizon r = r_s) after a finite proper time

  τ_horizon = X_1/c .                                         (2)

1.  What do the rocket observers see?

A photon that you emit at your proper time τ travels on a null line X–T = constant.  
Using (1) one finds that the interval in the rocket’s proper time Δτ_R between two successive photons emitted Δτ of your wrist-watch apart is

  Δτ_R = Δτ exp(aτ/c).                                        (3)

Hence the frequency they receive is

  f_R(τ) = f_emitted e^{–aτ/c}.                               (4)

So every tick of your clock arrives after a longer and longer interval; the telescope shows you ageing ever more slowly.  The red–shift becomes infinite (f_R → 0) as τ → τ_horizon.  Therefore

 • The rocket crew see you ageing increasingly slowly; your last observable tick is infinitely delayed.

2.  When do they see you cross the horizon?

In Rindler coordinates the horizon is ρ = 0, which corresponds to t → +∞.  Light that you emit an instant before crossing has to climb an infinite Rindler time to reach them, so no signal from you actually on or inside the horizon ever arrives.  Consequently

 • They never see you cross the horizon; to them you only approach it asymptotically.

3.  Can they wait and then rescue you?

Let the rocket dwell at ρ = ρ_0 until its own proper time τ_R = τ_d, and then fire its engines so as to drop freely (i.e. move on a new inertial straight line through the point (T_d , X_0) with slope < 1).

The rocket will intersect your world-line (1) only if that departure event lies in the past light-cone of some point on (1), i.e.

  X_0 – T_d  <  X_1 – T_1 .                                   (5)

Because X_0 – T_d decreases linearly with their waiting time while the right–hand side is fixed, (5) can be satisfied only up to a finite maximum delay τ_d^*.  If they hesitate longer than this, every timelike path they can follow stays outside the horizon and outside your world-line, so you are unreachable.

Thus

 • Yes, a rescue is possible in principle if they start soon enough;  
 • but after waiting a certain finite time (of order r_s/c for a solar-mass hole) it becomes impossible—no amount of later manoeuvring can make them catch up before you have crossed the horizon in your own finite proper time.

\boxed{%
\text{The telescope shows you ageing more and more slowly; the light from each tick is red-shifted exponentially. They never actually see you cross the horizon (the crossing would occur at }t=+\infty\text{ in their frame). If they decide quickly enough they can dive after you and pull you out, but after a finite delay the crossing has already happened for you and no rescue is any longer possible.} 

Confidence: 60%
